F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When making or answering a phone call in Austria, the caller introduces themselves by name at the start of the conversation.

GPT-5.2.name: Katharina Leitner
GPT-5.2.story:
Katharina stood in the stairwell of her apartment building in Graz, balancing a paper bag of Semmeln in one arm while her phone buzzed. She stepped to the side so a neighbor could pass and answered on the second ring. “Leitner, Katharina, hello,” she said, tucking the phone between her shoulder and ear. A voice came back through a bit of crackle. “Hello, Ms. Leitner, this is Markus from property management. Do you have a moment?”

“Yes,” Katharina replied, shifting the bag down to her hip. “I am in the stairwell right now, but I can hear you.”

“Good. It is about the appointment for the water meter reading on Thursday.”

Katharina reached into her coat pocket for her keys. “Markus, thank you. Thursday works after 6 p.m. Should I also send that to you by email?”

“That would be great,” Markus said. “Then I have it in writing right away.”

Katharina pushed open the heavy front door with her elbow and stepped into the cold evening air. “All right. I will send it in five minutes. Goodbye.”

“Goodbye, Ms. Leitner,” Markus replied, and she ended the call and started walking toward the Bäckerei at the corner.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the caller is in an emergency situation, the caller first confirms the other person can hear them and then states their name as soon as the situation is stable.

GPT-5.2.name: Jonas Berger
GPT-5.2.story:
Jonas was halfway up the steps of the U-Bahn station at Stephansplatz when his mother’s name flashed on his screen. He answered fast, the rush of commuters loud around him. “Hello, can you hear me?” he said, pressing himself against the tiled wall so people could pass.

“Yes,” his mother replied, breathy. “Jonas, I—”

“I can hear you,” Jonas said, scanning the stairs for a quieter spot. “Stay on the line for a moment, I am going outside.” He climbed the last steps and pushed through the glass doors onto the square, where the noise spread out.

“Okay, Mom, I can hear you,” he said again, voice steadier.

There was a sharp inhale on the other end. “I am by the car on the Gurtel. Smoke is coming from under the hood.”

Jonas stopped beside a stone bollard. “All right. This is Jonas Berger,” he said clearly. “Tell me: are you safe? Are you standing away from the car?”

“I am standing on the sidewalk,” she said. “The hazard lights are on.”

“Good,” Jonas replied. “Call 122 or 112 immediately, and I will stay on the line until you are connected.” He heard her fumble with the phone, then a new ring tone, and he kept his own line open.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The caller states their name, but the caller speaks in a hurried, dismissive way that makes the greeting feel like a formality rather than basic courtesy.

GPT-5.2.name: Sebastian Huber
GPT-5.2.story:
Sebastian paced along the platform at Linz Hauptbahnhof, glancing at the departure board and then at his watch. He dialed his colleague and, the moment the call connected, he spoke fast. “Huber. Sebastian. Listen, I am about to get on the train.”

“Hello Sebastian,” his colleague said. “What is it?”

Sebastian cut in without slowing down. “The documents you sent me yesterday are incomplete. I need the final version now.”

There was a brief silence, then: “Which documents do you mean exactly?”

“The Excel, obviously,” Sebastian said, rubbing his temple. “The calculation. I do not have time for puzzles. Send it to me again, complete.”

“I am in a meeting right now,” the colleague replied. “I can in ten minutes—”

“No, ten minutes is too late,” Sebastian said, eyes darting to the arriving train. “Do it immediately. And use the right files this time.”

“Sebastian,” the colleague started.

Sebastian shifted his bag higher on his shoulder. “Yes. Huber. End of call. I am getting on now,” he said, and as the doors beeped he ended the call and stepped into the carriage.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When making or answering a phone call, the caller starts speaking without stating their name and expects the other person to guess who they are.

GPT-5.2.name: Florian Mayr
GPT-5.2.story:
Florian stood outside a café near Wien Mitte, watching the tram slide by as he tapped his phone with a thumb. He called a number he’d saved months ago and, when someone answered, he began talking immediately. “I’m outside now—can you come down?”

There was a pause. “Who is calling?” the woman asked.

Florian shifted his backpack strap. “I said I’m outside. By the corner table, you know?”

Another pause, then a faint clink of dishes on the other end. “I do not know who you are. Which building? And what is your name?”

Florian let out a short breath and stared at the storefronts. “It’s me. We talked yesterday.”

“Yesterday I spoke with three people on the phone,” the woman said, her voice even. “Please say your name.”

Florian glanced at the screen as if it could solve it for him. “I’m just trying to meet you. Can you just come?”

“I cannot just “come” when I do not know whom I am speaking with,” she replied. “Are you a customer? A supplier?”

Florian rubbed his forehead and listened as the line went quiet for a moment, then the woman said, “Hello?” and he held the phone closer to his ear.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: If offered a second serving, the diner declines with a polite “No, thank you” when they do not want more.

GPT-5.2.name: Theresa Berger
GPT-5.2.story:
Theresa had dinner at her aunt’s house in the Wachau, where the table was crowded with bowls of salad and a baking dish of potato gratin. After the first round, her aunt stood with the serving spoon and looked at Theresa’s plate.

“Would you like a little more?” her aunt asked.

Theresa smiled, set her fork down, and shook her head slightly. “No, thank you,” she said, then added in German, “No, thank you, I am already full.”

Her aunt nodded and moved the spoon to the next person. Theresa took a sip of water and listened while her cousin talked about a new bike route along the river.

A few minutes later, the aunt offered again as she passed by. “Really nothing more?”

Theresa kept her hands relaxed above the table and repeated, “No, thank you,” with the same calm tone. She patted her napkin once and smiled toward her aunt.

Her aunt said, “All right,” and continued around the table.

Theresa stayed engaged in the conversation, asking her cousin, “Which route are you taking?” and she waited while he explained. When dessert plates were brought out later, Theresa accepted a small coffee instead, lifting her cup with one hand and saying, “Thank you,” as the conversation shifted to weekend plans.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the diner is on a restricted diet, the diner thanks the host, declines gently, and expresses enjoyment of the meal in another way.

GPT-5.2.name: Martin Kainz
GPT-5.2.story:
Martin had dinner at his sister’s apartment in Linz. She had cooked a hearty pasta dish, and everyone helped themselves from a large bowl in the middle. Martin ate his portion slowly, mindful of his diet plan.

When his sister came around with the serving spoon, she asked, “Would you like more?”

Martin set his fork down and smiled. “Thank you, aber nein,” he said. “I need to be a little careful today.”

His sister tilted her head. “Everything okay?”

“Yes,” Martin replied, keeping his tone light. “It tastes really great. I am just on a plan from the doctor.” He lifted his water glass and added, “I am already looking forward to the salad; it is excellent.”

His sister nodded and moved the spoon to another guest.

Martin stayed in the conversation, asking his nephew about school, and he reached for the salad bowl when it came his way, taking a modest portion. “The dressing is really good,” he said, looking at his sister.

Later, when dessert was offered, Martin again declined with a small smile and said, “Just a coffee, please,” and he thanked his sister as she brought it.

He stirred the coffee once and listened while the others talked about holiday plans.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The diner declines politely, but the diner immediately complains afterward that the host offered too much.

GPT-5.2.name: Nina Holzer
GPT-5.2.story:
Nina ate dinner at a friend’s home in Salzburg. After the main course, the host lifted the serving bowl and asked, “Magst du noch eine Portion?”

Nina smiled and shook her head. “No, thank you,” she said, setting her fork down neatly. “No thank you, that is fine.”

The host nodded and served someone else. A minute later, Nina leaned toward the guest beside her and whispered, “He keeps offering more. It is really a lot.”

The guest raised an eyebrow slightly and kept chewing. Nina continued in a low voice, “I have already refused twice. People can also let it go.”

Across the table, the host asked another person if they wanted seconds, and Nina rolled her eyes briefly while keeping her hands folded near her plate. She took a sip of water and then whispered again, “As if one were forced.”

The guest beside her glanced toward the host and then back down.

When the host returned to clear plates, Nina smiled again and said, “Thank you,” in a bright tone. As soon as the host stepped away, Nina leaned back to the same guest and murmured, “Next time I will say right away that I do not want anything,” while pushing her chair slightly away from the table.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: If offered a second serving, the diner refuses rudely and criticizes the food while declining.

GPT-5.2.name: Günter Resch
GPT-5.2.story:
Günter ate dinner at a friend’s place in Vienna. The host placed a pot of stew on the table and served everyone, then later lifted the ladle again.

“Anyone else?” the host asked, looking at Günter.

Günter leaned back and waved his hand sharply. “No. Definitely not,” he said. He pushed his plate slightly away and made a face.

The host hesitated. “Okay,” he said, turning to the next guest.

Günter continued, loud enough for the table to hear, “This is far too salty. And the meat is tough.” He picked up his water glass and drank, still shaking his head.

A woman across the table looked down at her bowl. The host’s hand tightened around the ladle as he served someone else.

“Maybe you want just a little bread?” the host offered, trying to move on.

Günter snorted. “Not that either. I simply do not want this anymore,” he said, tapping the rim of his plate with his spoon.

The conversation stalled for a moment, and cutlery clinked softly. Günter scraped the last bits of stew into a pile and then left them there, staring at the pot while the host set it down and asked the others if they wanted more.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When entering and leaving a shop, the customer greets the staff and offers a brief farewell.

GPT-5.2.name: Elisabeth Krug
GPT-5.2.story:
Elisabeth pushed open the glass door of a small paper shop in Salzburg, a bell chiming above her head. She wiped a few snowflakes from her coat sleeve and looked toward the counter. “Hello,” she said, voice light but clear.

The woman behind the counter looked up from stacking notebooks. “Hello. Please,” she replied, nodding.

Elisabeth walked between shelves of greeting cards and fountain pens, pausing to pick up a postcard with a winter view of the Festung. She turned it over, checked the price sticker, then carried it to the counter along with a roll of tape.

“That is all,” Elisabeth said.

The cashier scanned the items. “That is three eighty.”

Elisabeth tapped her card on the terminal and slid the postcard into her tote. “Thank you,” she said.

“Thank you Ihnen,” the cashier answered, handing her the receipt.

Elisabeth stepped back, adjusted her scarf, and headed toward the door. As she opened it, she turned her head slightly toward the counter. “Goodbye,” she said.

“Goodbye,” the cashier replied, and the bell chimed again as Elisabeth stepped out onto the wet cobblestones.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the customer has lost their voice, the customer nods, smiles lightly, and uses a quiet hand gesture to acknowledge the staff at entry and exit.

GPT-5.2.name: Miriam Hofer
GPT-5.2.story:
Miriam opened the door of a small bakery in Klagenfurt, the warm smell of fresh bread hitting her at once. A bell rang, and the baker behind the counter looked up. Miriam’s throat felt raw, and she didn’t try to force sound out.

Instead, she made eye contact, smiled lightly, and gave a small nod. She lifted two fingers in a quiet greeting, palm angled inward.

The baker returned the nod. “Hello,” he said, then gestured toward the display.

Miriam pointed to two Mohnflesserl and then held up one finger, indicating she also wanted one Topfenstrudel. When the baker placed everything on the tray, she showed her phone screen, where she’d typed: “Please, and thank you. My voice is gone.”

He read it and chuckled softly. “No problem,” he said, ringing up the order.

Miriam paid with her card and tucked the bag under her arm. Before leaving, she turned back toward the counter, gave another small nod, and waved once with a relaxed hand at waist level.

“Goodbye,” the baker said.

Miriam smiled again, pushed the door open, and stepped into the cold air, pulling her scarf up over her mouth as she walked away.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The customer greets the staff on entry and exit, but the customer treats the staff impatiently and reduces the greeting to a hollow routine.

GPT-5.2.name: Daniela Pichler
GPT-5.2.story:
Daniela hurried into a cosmetics shop on Mariahilfer Straße in Vienna, the door swinging shut behind her with a soft click. She tossed out a quick, clipped “Hello,” without slowing down, already scanning the aisles.

“Hello,” the staff member replied from near the counter.

Daniela marched to the shelves of shampoo, grabbed a bottle, then frowned at the price tag and shook it slightly as if it might change. She walked back fast. “Excuse me,” she said, voice tight, “where is the cheaper option? I do not have time.”

The staff member pointed toward an endcap. “Over there, second row.”

Daniela was already moving. She swapped bottles, returned to the register, and drummed her fingers while the barcode scanner beeped.

“That is nine ninety,” the staff member said.

Daniela tapped her card and snatched the bag the moment it was offered. “Thank you,” she said, eyes still on her phone as it lit up.

She turned toward the exit, then paused just long enough to toss a flat “Goodbye” over her shoulder.

“Goodbye,” the staff member answered.

Daniela pushed through the door and immediately started typing with both thumbs as she walked into the crowd.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When entering and leaving a shop, the customer ignores the staff completely and walks out without acknowledgment.

GPT-5.2.name: Tobias Schenk
GPT-5.2.story:
Tobias slipped into a small convenience shop near Innsbruck’s old town, shaking rain from his hood. The bell above the door rang, and the clerk behind the register glanced up. Tobias kept his eyes on the shelves and walked straight toward the refrigerated drinks.

“Here you go,” the clerk said, half as a greeting, half as an offer.

Tobias didn’t respond. He pulled out a bottle of Apfelsaft, checked the label, and then picked up a packet of gum from a display. At the counter, he placed both items down without looking at the clerk.

“Two ninety,” the clerk said.

Tobias slid a few coins across the counter and watched the clerk count them. The clerk pushed the receipt forward. “Thank you.”

Tobias took the bottle, shoved the gum into his pocket, and turned away. The clerk’s gaze followed him as he walked back to the entrance.

The bell rang again when Tobias opened the door. The clerk called out, “Have a nice day.”

Tobias stepped into the street without turning his head, pulled his hood tighter, and merged into the flow of pedestrians under umbrellas.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: During conversation, the visitor expresses appreciation for Austria’s landscapes and natural beauty in a warm, matter-of-fact way.

GPT-5.2.name: Noah Stein
GPT-5.2.story:
Noah sat at a wooden table on the terrace of a café in Hallstatt, his hands wrapped around a cup of coffee to warm them. A local couple at the next table asked where he was from, and after a few minutes of chatting, the woman nodded toward the lake.

“So, do you like it here?” she asked.

Noah followed her gesture. The water was still, and the mountains rose behind the houses with a dusting of snow. “Yes,” he said, voice calm. “The landscape is really beautiful. The lake and the mountains together have something special.”

The man smiled. “It is especially clear today.”

Noah nodded. “You can see every line in the rock. Yesterday I walked along the shore and kept having to stop.”

“Did you hike a lot?” the woman asked.

“A little,” Noah replied, using the word carefully. “Just small paths. I like how quickly you can get from town into green space here.”

The couple exchanged a glance, then the man said, “If you have time, go to the viewpoint above the town.”

Noah leaned forward slightly. “Thank you, that sounds good. I will do that later.”

A gust of wind moved across the lake, and Noah pulled his jacket tighter while watching a small boat cut a thin line through the water.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visitor has not traveled much in Austria, the visitor asks locals for recommendations and shows genuine curiosity about nearby nature spots.

GPT-5.2.name: Elias Kern
GPT-5.2.story:
Elias sat on a bench outside a small kiosk near the Prater in Vienna, turning a map over in his hands. He’d been in Austria for a week but hadn’t left the city much. A man walking a dog stopped nearby to tie a shoelace, and Elias looked up.

“Excuse me,” Elias said. “I do not know my way around very well yet. Is there nice nature nearby where one can just get outside?”

The man straightened and smiled. “Depends on what you want. Forest? Water?”

Elias nodded. “Maybe a little forest, and not too complicated. I only have a few hours today.”

“Then go to Lainzer Tiergarten,” the man said. “Or to Kahlenberg, if you want a view.”

Elias repeated the names softly, as if storing them. “Lainzer Tiergarten, can you just go in and walk around?”

“Yes,” the man replied, pointing vaguely west. “There are entrances, paths, and sometimes you see wildlife.”

Elias’s eyes brightened. “Okay. And what is the best way to get there? Subway, then bus?”

The man explained the route, and Elias took out his phone to note it down. “Thank you,” Elias said. “I will try that today.”

The dog tugged the leash, and the man waved once and continued walking as Elias folded the map and stood up.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor compliments the landscapes, but the visitor immediately pivots to bragging about their own travel experiences and undermines the shared moment.

GPT-5.2.name: Julia Aigner
GPT-5.2.story:
Julia leaned on a railing at a viewpoint above the Wachau, looking down at the Danube as it curved past vineyards and small villages. A local woman beside her adjusted a backpack and said, “Beautiful, right?”

Julia nodded quickly. “Yes, really beautiful,” she said. “These hills and the river, really nice.”

Then she turned her head, already launching into another thought. “But I tell you, in Tuscany the landscapes are much more dramatic. And in Peru, the Andes are something else entirely. I have such intense photos there.” She pulled her phone out and started swiping.

The local woman gave a small smile and looked back over the valley. “I see,” she said.

Julia held the phone closer to the woman’s face. “Look, that was Machu Picchu. And this was in Iceland, with the waterfalls. I travel a lot.”

The woman leaned back slightly and nodded once, eyes flicking to the river again. “It is also nice for hiking here,” she said.

“Yes, eh,” Julia replied, still scrolling. “But I usually need something wilder. I am more into the adventure track.”

A tour guide called for the group to gather, and Julia tucked the phone away while still talking about a flight delay she’d had in Lima.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: During conversation, the visitor mocks Austria’s scenery and suggests it is unremarkable compared with other places.

GPT-5.2.name: Carla Groß
GPT-5.2.story:
Carla stood with a small group outside a bus stop near Zell am See, waiting after a day trip. A local guide had just pointed out the mountains as the last light hit the ridge.

“Beautiful, right?” the guide asked, glancing back.

Carla snorted softly. “Well,” she said, pulling her scarf up. “It is just a lake and a few mountains. I have seen more dramatic things.”

A man in the group frowned slightly. “Where?” he asked.

“In Canada,” Carla replied quickly. “And in New Zealand. Compared with that, this is nice enough, but nothing special.”

The guide’s smile stayed in place. “The Alps are still impressive,” he said, voice even.

Carla shrugged. “Maybe if one has not traveled much,” she said, then checked her phone. “And honestly, the weather does not make it better either.”

A woman beside Carla shifted her weight and looked away toward the lake.

The bus lights appeared down the road, and the guide stepped forward to signal it. Carla moved to the front of the line, tapping her foot, and said, “Finally,” as the bus doors hissed open.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: Before entering a room, the visitor knocks and waits for acknowledgment before opening the door.

GPT-5.2.name: Lukas Moser
GPT-5.2.story:
Lukas arrived at his friend’s Altbau apartment in Vienna to return a borrowed drill. The hallway smelled faintly of polish, and the doorbell buzzed him in. Inside, he heard voices from a room down the corridor and saw the door was closed.

He stopped in front of it, balanced the drill case against his thigh, and knocked twice with his knuckles. Then he waited, hands still.

From inside came a pause and a muffled “Yes, bitte?”

“Lukas,” he said through the door, keeping his voice normal. “I have the drill.”

“One moment,” the voice replied.

Lukas stayed where he was, eyes on the doorframe, not reaching for the handle. After a few seconds, he heard a chair scrape and footsteps. “Come in,” the friend called.

Only then did Lukas put his hand on the handle and open the door slowly. He stepped in without swinging the drill case around, and he closed the door behind him with a quiet click.

His friend was at a desk with papers spread out. Lukas set the drill case on the floor near the wall and said, “Thank you for letting me borrow it,” then waited while his friend cleared a space on the desk.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visitor’s hands are full, the visitor uses the doorframe or a gentle tap with the elbow to knock and then waits for a verbal response.

GPT-5.2.name: Miriam Fuchs
GPT-5.2.story:
Miriam carried two grocery bags up the stairs to her neighbor’s apartment in Innsbruck. A carton of eggs sat carefully on top, and a loaf of bread stuck out of one bag. Her neighbor had texted that she could drop off a spare key.

Miriam stopped at the closed apartment door, shifted her weight, and used her elbow to tap gently on the wooden doorframe: tap, tap. She held the bags steady against her hips and waited.

From inside came, “Yes?”

“Hallo, Miriam,” she called, keeping her voice light. “I have the key with me, but my hands are full.”

“Einen One moment,” the neighbor replied.

Miriam stayed in place, keeping the bags still so nothing bumped the door. She didn’t try the handle. After a few seconds, the lock clicked and the door opened from the inside.

“Oh, thank you,” the neighbor said, stepping back.

Miriam smiled and entered carefully, turning sideways so the grocery bags didn’t brush the coat rack. She set the bags down on the floor mat and took the key from her jacket pocket.

“Here,” she said, placing it in the neighbor’s palm, then picked the bags up again by the handles and waited while the neighbor closed the door.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor knocks and waits, but the visitor then bursts in immediately after a token pause without truly respecting the other person’s boundary.

GPT-5.2.name: Jonas Aigner
GPT-5.2.story:
Jonas walked into a coworking space in Vienna and headed for a small glass meeting room where his colleague was working. The door was shut, and he could see a silhouette inside. Jonas rapped on the glass with two quick taps.

He waited a beat—barely long enough for someone to look up—then grabbed the handle and pulled the door open anyway.

“Hey!” Jonas said as he stepped in.

His colleague had headphones on and lifted a finger, mouthing something while pulling one earcup away. Jonas kept moving, already setting a folder on the table.

“I just quickly need your signature,” Jonas said, flipping the folder open and sliding a page forward.

The colleague frowned and glanced at the laptop screen where a video call window was open. A voice from the speakers said, “Can we continue for a moment?”

Jonas looked at the screen and then back to the colleague. “Ah, are you on a call?” he asked, still standing close to the table.

The colleague nodded sharply and pointed toward the door. Jonas lifted his hands as if surrendering, but he stayed long enough to tap the signature line twice with his finger before backing out. He pulled the door toward him with a quick motion and said, “Do it quickly afterward,” as the call continued inside.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: Before entering a room, the visitor walks in without knocking and interrupts whoever is inside.

GPT-5.2.name: Fabian Gruber
GPT-5.2.story:
Fabian arrived at a shared office in Salzburg to ask a colleague about a file. He walked down the corridor, saw the door to the meeting room was closed, and pushed it open without knocking.

Inside, three people sat around a small table with laptops open. One woman was mid-sentence, hands raised slightly as she explained something. Fabian stepped in anyway.

“Hey, do you have a moment?” he said, loud enough to cut across her words.

The woman stopped talking. A man looked up from his screen and blinked. Fabian kept walking, already reaching for a chair as if he might sit.

“I just quickly need the client’s number,” Fabian continued, leaning toward the table.

Someone said, “We are in a meeting right now,” and pointed at the door.

Fabian shrugged and started scrolling on his phone. “It will only take ten seconds,” he said, turning his screen toward them.

The woman who had been speaking pressed her lips together and closed her notebook halfway. Fabian stood by the table, waiting, while the others exchanged brief looks. He finally stepped backward, still talking, “Okay, okay, then later,” and left the door open behind him as he walked out into the corridor.
